sub_pixel_system.notes: No sub-pixel system documented for this puzzle game

tetromino_fall_speeds.description: Drop speed in frames per grid cell by level (NTSC/North America vs PAL/Europe-Asia)
tetromino_fall_speeds.notes: Level 29 is the 'kill screen' - pieces fall at 1 grid cell per frame, intended to be unplayable. Speed differences account for PAL vs NTSC frame rates.

special_mechanics.name: Tetromino Placement
special_mechanics.description: Tetrominoes fall in random order with preview window showing next piece. Players can move left/right and rotate pieces before placement.

special_mechanics.name: A-Type Mode
special_mechanics.description: Score accumulation mode. Level advances every 10 lines cleared. Game speed increases at levels 1-10, then again at levels 13, 16, 19, and 29.

physics_values.scoring_formula: Single: 40(n+1), Double: 100(n+1), Triple: 300(n+1), Tetris: 1200(n+1) where n = current level

special_mechanics.name: B-Type Mode
special_mechanics.description: Challenge mode with randomized obstacle blocks at bottom. Goal is to clear 25 lines at constant selected level.

special_mechanics.name: Roll Technique
special_mechanics.description: Modern high-level strategy where players rapidly roll the controller back to move tetrominoes faster than normal input, enabling play past level 29.

physics_values.enabled_by: Controller hardware properties, not intentional game design
physics_values.effect: Enables extended play sessions and novel speed techniques

feel_descriptors: deterministic piece selection with random order
tier_notes: Tier 2 (qualitative/empirical). This is a puzzle game without traditional physics mechanics. No sub-pixel system, gravity, or jump mechanics exist. Only tetromino fall speed data is documented and quantified. Regional differences (NTSC vs PAL) affect perceived game speed.
sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Tetris (NES, Nintendo)
